text: I come here every Tuesday morning with my friend Peggy and it is simply the best start to the week. The decaf is smooth and never bitter, the scones are fresh, and the room is wonderfully quiet. The staff recognize us now and have our usual ready. A true gem in Glastonbury.

Lovely little bakery with a calm atmosphere. The almond croissants are delicate and not too sweet. Plenty of seating and the noise level stays low even on busy mornings. Perfect for a quiet catch-up with a friend.

Very pleasant bakery. The butterscotch muffins are wonderful. I appreciate that they offer decaf drip coffee — so many places only do espresso drinks now. Gets a little busy around 9 but settles down by 10:30.
The cinnamon tea cakes here are extraordinary. Gentle flavors, no heavy spices. The room is softly lit with real wood tables. One of the few places in Glastonbury that feels genuinely restful.

Finally, a soup restaurant that understands gentle flavors. The roasted butternut squash soup is magnificent — mild, creamy, and warming. No tomato-heavy options dominating the menu. Quiet dining room, good for a peaceful lunch. I will be back.
A lovely spot for a light lunch. The parsnip and leek soup was comforting and not at all spicy. The bread served alongside was soft and buttery. Small dining room keeps things cozy and quiet.

Best soup in the Hartford area by far. The sweet potato bisque is silky and perfectly seasoned — nothing too sharp or acidic. Staff are attentive and unobtrusive. The tables are well spaced for quiet conversation.
Nice selection of daily soups and hearty bread. The mushroom barley was earthy and deeply satisfying. A calm, unhurried atmosphere that makes it easy to linger over a book.

We asked specifically about dishes without heavy tomato sauce and the server was knowledgeable and helpful. The linguine with brown butter and sage was exquisite — light, fragrant, and easy on the stomach. Quiet corner tables available. Harriet and I will make this a regular.

Wonderful Italian cooking with a lighter hand than most. The pappardelle with truffle cream was refined and not overwhelming. The dining room is elegant but not pretentious, and the sound level stays pleasantly low.
Asked about GERD-friendly options and the kitchen happily accommodated. The risotto with roasted fennel was outstanding. Staff do not rush you. Lovely evening out.

Good portions, refined presentation. The gnocchi with butter and herbs is standout. Menu clearly marks spicy dishes which is very helpful. Noise level is very manageable — can hold a real conversation without strain.

A true tearoom experience — proper loose-leaf teas, small sandwiches with soft fillings, and a genuinely tranquil atmosphere. The chamomile and peppermint selections are excellent. No background music blaring. I felt completely at ease.
Delightful afternoon tea. The scones are buttery and the cucumber sandwiches are fresh. Everything is served at a gentle pace. Just what Glastonbury needed.

An absolutely lovely farm-to-table spot. The roasted carrot and herb soup changed my mind about dining out on weeknights. The staff are warm and the lighting is soft. Not a hint of loud music. I checked the menu online first and there are plenty of mild, gentle options.
Everything sourced locally and the flavors show it. The chilled cucumber soup was perfect on a warm afternoon. Very quiet dining room — ideal for a relaxed conversation. Will bring my book club here.
Outstanding. The butternut squash ravioli with sage brown butter is one of the best things I have eaten this year. Quiet, understated decor. Service is attentive without hovering.

Very pleasant new American cuisine. I had the roasted chicken with root vegetables and it was just right — nothing acidic, nothing spicy. The dining room is large but the booths muffle noise well. Harriet and I both left feeling content.
Solid menu with thoughtful seasonal choices. The roasted beet and walnut salad was beautiful and earthy. Staff were knowledgeable about dietary needs. Noise level is comfortable for conversation.

A lovely dinner spot. The pan-seared halibut with herb butter is exquisite and very gentle on the palate. No heavy sauces. The room stays quiet even on a Friday. Genuinely one of the nicest tables in Glastonbury.
A wonderfully quiet room, which matters to me — I cannot abide loud restaurant music. The decaf was smooth and the service unhurried. (visit January 2025)
Peggy and I come here often. The staff are kind, the tables well-spaced, and the menu has gentle, GERD-friendly options. (visit January 2026)
I appreciate a place that lets you linger over a book. Comfortable, calm, and the soup was excellent on a cold day. (visit January 2025)
Good, honest food without fuss. Harriet and I had a lovely lunch and the noise level was blessedly low. (visit January 2026)
The butternut squash soup rivaled my own, which is high praise. Warm room, worn-in and welcoming. (visit January 2025)
Reserved New England charm — nothing showy, everything done well. We'll be back. (visit January 2026)
They had decaf ready and the staff remembered our usual. A true neighborhood gem. (visit February 2025)
Quiet corner, patient service, and a menu mindful of plainer tastes. Exactly what I want. (visit February 2026)
